The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: CHAPTER 1, SOCIAL STRUCTURE, STRATIFICATION AND SOCIAL, PROCESSES IN SOCIETY, INTRODUCTION. full_notes:
Social Structure Stratification and Social Processes is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that leisure opportunities s/he avails, the health access s/he has, i.e. her/his lifestyle in general.

The text explains that as in the case of social structure, social stratification constrains individual action.

The text explains that one of the central concerns of the sociological perspective has been to understand the dialectical relationship between the individual and society.

The text explains that you will recall C.Wright Mill’s elaboration of the sociological imagination that seeks to unfold the interplay between an individual’s biography and society’s history.

The text explains that it is towards understanding this dialectical relationship between the society and individual that we need to discuss the three central concepts of structure, stratification and social processes in this chapter.

The text explains that in the next few chapters we then move on to how social structure in rural and urban societies are different, to broader relationships between environment and society.

The text explains that in the last two chapters we look at western social thinkers and Indian sociologists and their writings that would help us further understand the ideas of social structure, stratification as well as social processes.

The text explains that cHAPTER 1 SOCIAL STRUCTURE, STRATIFICATION AND SOCIAL PROCESSES IN SOCIETY INTRODUCTION You will recall that the earlier book Introducing Sociology, Class XI had begun with a discussion on the relationship between personal problems and social issues.

The text explains that we also saw how individuals are located within collectivities such as groups, classes, gender, castes and tribes.

The text explains that indeed each of you, is a member of not just one kind of collectivity, but many overlapping ones.

The text explains that for instance, you are a member of your own peer group, your family and kin, your class and gender, your country and region.

The text explains that each individual thus has a specific location in the social structure and social stratification system (see pages 28-35 in Introducing Sociology).

The text explains that this also implies that they have different levels and types of access to social resources.

The text explains that in other words the choices an individual has in life in terms of the school s/he goes to — or if s/he goes to school at all — would depend on the social s t r a t u m t h a t s/ h e b e l o n g s t o .

The text explains that likewise with the clothes s/he gets to wear, the food s/he consumes, the

The text explains that 2 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY regularities that the concept of social structure refers.

The text explains that upto a point, it is helpful to think of the structural characteristics of societies as resembling the structure of a building.

The text explains that social structures are made up of human actions and relationships.

The text explains that thus, the ideas of social reproduction and social structure are very closely related to one another in sociological analysis.

The text explains that for example, consider a school and a family structure.

The text explains that for as we saw for ourselves human beings in schools or families do bring changes The central question that this chapter seeks to discuss is to what extent the individual constrained by, and to what extent s/he is free of, the social structure?

The text explains that to what extent does one’s position in society or location in the stratification system gover n individual choice?

The text explains that do social structure and social stratification influence the manner people act?

The text explains that in this chapter we deal briefly with the terms ‘social structure’ and ‘social stratification’.

The text explains that you have already discussed social stratification in some detail in Chapter 2 of the earlier book Introducing Sociology, Class XI (NCERT, 2006).

The text explains that we then move on to focus on three social processes namely; cooperation, competition and conflict.

The text explains that in dealing with each of these processes we shall try and see how social structure and stratification impinge themselves on the social processes.

The text explains that in other words how individuals and groups cooperate, compete and conflict depending upon their position within the social structure and stratification system.

The text explains that sOCIAL STRUCTURE AND STRATIFICATION The term social structure points to the fact that society is structured — i.e., organised or arranged — in particular ways.

The text explains that 3 SOCIAL STRUCTURE, STRATIFICATION AND SOCIAL PROCESSES IN SOCIETY Different types of buildings in rural and urban areas

The text explains that 4 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY to reproduce the structure even while introducing changes.

The text explains that and as we shall find later in this chapter, cooperation can be enforced and thereby serve to conceal conflict.

The text explains that durkheim argued that society has primacy over the individual person.

The text explains that society is far more than the sum of individual acts; it has a ‘firmness’ or ‘solidity’ comparable to structures in the material environment.

The text explains that the structure of the room constrains the range of his or her possible activities.

The text explains that social structure, according to Durkheim, constrains our activities in a parallel way, setting limits to what we can do as individuals.

The text explains that other social thinkers like Karl Marx would emphasise the constraints of social structure but would at the same time stress human creativity or agency to both reproduce and change social structure.

The text explains that marx argued that human beings make history, but not as they wish to or in conditions of their choice, but within the constraints and possibilities of the historical and structural situation that they are in.

The text explains that to recall the concept of social strati-fication in Chapter 2 of Introducing Sociology, Class XI, Social stratification refers to the existence of structured inequalities between groups in society, in terms of their a c c e s s t o m a t e r i a l o r s y m b o l i c Activity 1 Discuss with your grandparents and others of that generation to find out about the ways in which families/schools have changed and the ways in which they have remained the same.

The text explains that in other words, can you describe the structure of your family?

The text explains that discuss with your teachers how they understand the school as a structure.

The text explains that do students, teachers and the staff have to act in certain ways to maintain or reproduce the structure?

The text explains that as the division of labour becomes more complex, people become more and more dependent on one another, because each person needs goods and services that those in other occupations supply.

The text explains that relationships of economic reciprocity and mutual dependency come to replace shared beliefs in creating social consensus.

The text explains that social Constraint: A term referring to the fact that the groups and societies of which we are a part exert a conditioning influence on our behaviour.

The text explains that social constraint was regarded by Durkheim as one of the distinctive properties of ‘social facts’.

The text explains that structures: Refers generally to constructed frameworks and patterns of organisation, which in some way constrain or direct human behaviour.

The text explains that a Free Press (Paperback), The MacMillan Company, New York.

The text explains that controversies in Sociology: A Canadian Introduction.

The text explains that 1990. “Gender and Cooperative Conflicts” in Persistent Inequalities (ed) II.Tinker, pp.123-49.

The text explains that charisma, Stability and Change; An Analysis of Bhoodan- Gramdan Movement in India.

The text explains that arguing With the Crocodile, Gender and Class in Bangladesh, Zed Books, London.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society, Chapter 1: Social Structure Stratification and Social Processes

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Social Structure Stratification and Social Processes, which statement is supported by the source text about 2 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY?
options: Upto a point, it is helpful to think of the structural characteristics of societies as resembling the structure of a building.
A building has walls, a floor and a roof, which together give it a particular ‘shape’ or form (Giddens 2004: 667).
But the metaphor can be a very misleading one if applied too strictly.
Social structures are made up of human actions and relationships.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Social Structure Stratification and Social Processes, which statement is supported by the source text about SOCIAL STRUCTURE AND STRATIFICATION?
options: What gives these their patterning is their repetition across periods of time and distances of space.
Thus, the ideas of social reproduction and social structure are very closely related to one another in sociological analysis.
In a school certain ways of behaving are repeated over the years and become institutions.
For instance admission procedures, codes of conduct, annual functions, daily assemblies and in some cases even school anthems.

options: Likewise in families certain ways of behaving, marriage practices, notions of relationships, duties and expectations are set.
Even as old members of the family or school may pass away and new members enter, the institution goes on.
Yet we also know that changes do take place within the family and in schools.
The above discussion and activity should help us understand human societies as buildings that are at every moment being reconstructed by the very bricks...
